full_text:
Färkkilä lab

REMEMBER TO FILL THE LOG-BOOK!!!!The basics

Preparing for Scanning

Ensure that the instrument is turned on at least 30 minutes before starting the scanning process.

Never leave a needle in the system. Always remove the needle before scanning.

Using RCIC Application

Double-click the RCIC icon to launch the application. It is the only way to check if the instrument is connected to the computer.

Check the color code on the interface. If it is flashing or not connected, make sure the connections are properly established. When everything is blue, the instrument is ready to scan.

Using Imager

The CytePicker II can be used as a normal microscope, allowing you to visualize samples directly. You can also image with these settings

Adjust the imaging settings as required for your authentication needs.
(change Z if you do not see anything)

The instrument supports various channels. Refer to the following color codes:

BV421: DAPI (blue)

A488: FITC (green)

SYTOX Orange: TRITC (orange)

Red: 647 nm

750: Utilized in specific circumstances

The system provides five good channels and two unusual channels.

if the imager window closes/ is not open: “image main controls - view - imager”

Create Assay

To create a new assay, follow these steps:

Open the CyteHub interface and navigate to the assay section.

Click on "New Assay" to initiate the creation process.

Within the assay, adjust the imaging settings, (binning of 1 merges pixels and reduces resolution)

Choose the magnification for imaging, typically 20x or 40x H (high magnification).

Do not change the values for the X and Y overlap, which are typically set to 20 um each.

Nominal Z values can be changed only in specific scenarios. -30 is recommended for most HGSC samples.

Add the targets and exposure time: the recommended exposure time for imaging is 100ms, but you can adjust it as per your specific requirements (do not overexpose it, the LUTs range should not be near the max value of 65,000).

Steps for imaging in CyteHub

CyteHub provides a comprehensive view of the entire environment, including scan setup, scan area definition, cell results, tissue results, and assay settings.

Login to CyteHub using your credentials.

In the scan setup, select your assay.

Choose how you want to use the detected scan area: Auto or Manual. If this is the first time you scan this slide choose manual, otherwise click “use the saved area for repeating scans with the same coordinates”, you can keep the Auto mode in this case.

Make sure the assay remains the same as you used last time to automatically reuse coordinates (same scan area).

If you have multiple slides, enter their IDs one by one, clicking "Enter" after each entry.

Setting up the area of scanning during your first scan

To set up the area for imaging in your first scan, follow these steps:

Scan Setup in CyteHub

Access CyteHub and navigate to the scan setup section, fill it as previously described (project assay + manual + redetect)

Go to CyteFinder and select the slide you want to image and run the scan

Rename the selected slide as per your preference.

Ensure that the slide is properly positioned for imaging.

Do Not Close the Window

It is crucial to keep the scan window open throughout the scanning process.

Closing the window may interrupt the scan or result in data loss.

Scan Area

Once the first scan in low magnification is ready proceed to the scan area section within CyteHub.

Click on Edit region. Adjust the rectangle to define the desired area of interest (ROI) for imaging.

Modify the size and position of the rectangle as needed to encompass the target region.

NOTE: DO NOT INCLUDE THE SPACER BORDERS IN YOUR REGION

Assign Focus Points and Register

To ensure proper focus during scanning, assign focus points in the desired order.

Assign the necessary points to accurately specify the regions of the ROI. NOTE: SELECT A LOT OF POINTS (20-50) in big slides. Put points even in regions outside the rectangle of scanning, but in areas where there is tissue.

Double-click on the ROI visualization to assign it as a focus point.

Once all focus points are assigned, click on the "Register" button to confirm the focus settings.

CyteFinder - Control the Start of the Scanning

Utilize CyteFinder again to manage the initiation of the scanning process.

Set the appropriate slot for scanning within CyteFinder based on your experimental requirements.

Scan with Imager [not recommended]

Low Exposure UV Channel: Use the UV channel with low exposure to prevent tissue bleaching during the setup process. (low exposure = 0.0025)

Discover Tissue with 4X Magnification: Start with 4X magnification to locate your tissue. Go to the scan menu.

Select Area of Interest: Once you have identified your tissue, switch to 20X (or the desired objective) magnification. Right-click and draw a rectangle around your area of interest. Take note of the coordinates for future reference.

Note Coordinates: Make a note of the coordinates to ensure you can input them accurately in the next cycle.

Set Output Folder and File Name: Select the output folder and specify a name for your file in the Scan output folder.

Adjust Focus Points: Click on the wheel and choose "Edit Focus Points." Delete any existing points in the Imager Point List panel. Drag your rectangle to position the desired focus points. Click the "+" button to add focus points and save them. You can export these focus points for future use. Remember to check the folder where you saved them.

Select Channels and Exposure Time: In the control tab, choose the channels you want to scan and set the desired exposure time for each channel. The first channel is used for autofocus.

Prefocus + Scan: Click on "Prefocus + Scan" to initiate the process. The resulting files in your designated folder will be .rcpnl and .metadata files.

Subsequent Cycles: For subsequent cycles, refer to your previous coordinates from your notes since they cannot be exported. Go to the wheel and select "Edit Focus Points." Delete existing points in the Imager Point List. Import your focus points from the previously exported file. Add additional focus points if needed and ensure that the exposure times for your channels are suitable. Click "Prefocus + Scan" to continue the scanning process.

Troubleshooting: common Q&As

My ome.tiffs are not generating after the scans!:You have scanned with antibodies name with space :eg: Tigit2 is fine but not TIGIT 2 is not . OR this is because the disk is too full or even if you transfer your files right now to a hard drive the problem will persist. You can stitch them manually to check the scan or open the rcpnl with FIJI to check the overall focus quality.

My scan is aborted and the error Barcode not associated with any scan job appears: you have either not defined the coordinates for that specific slide or most likely you have not been consistent with the naming. Check also you did not add any space after your file name, that is considered another character.

My slide looks so blurry! : check that the slide is correctly in place and the tissue is facing down.

Modify nominal z in the assay and repeat the scan:

use 0/-30,-60 depending on the case

if the error persist contact the cycif person

I just ended my scan and it does not show in .rcpnl/tissue/ folder: this is normal, only the .rcpnls that have been processed and whose ome tiffs have been stitched appear here.

Error: Focus error occurred due to insufficient number of focus points in defined scan area. Increase the number of focus points . Go to the scan area and choose more ROI’s. Or increase the focus channel exposure time.

Picking

To perform picking using the CytePicker II system, follow these steps:

Ensure that the imager is properly set up.

Check “Settings” of CytePicker in the Imager: Main Controls.

Remember to ALWAYS use 10x magnification for the imaging beforehand.

Access the CytePicker tab within the software.

Set the “right” to “needle holder” in the Imager Main Control field instead of “SuperFrost Plus”.

Place the tray and slide on the instrument.

Check if “Set Needle type” corresponds to your used needle (40 µm)

Take a Needle

Click on the needle coordinates and align them with the hole on the tray.

Click on "Load the Needle" from the control panel above to load the needle into position (Needle Loader panel).

Check and Change Needle

Verify the condition of the needle by opening the slide holder space with “Load Slides”. The position of the needle should look like the picture.

If necessary, change the needle.

Flush

Click on the rinsing solution in the software interface.

center needle above the tube before you flush (this also accounts for retrieval, waste, deposit)

Select "Flush Needle" to flush the needle with the solution.

flush several times to clean needle thoroughly

Calibration

Place the empty calibration slide in the left slide holder position.

Use (if possible) a new calibration slide regularly

during calibration needle never touches the slide

Manual Calibration:

Move the needle to the “top” position in the Imager: CytePicker2 Manual Controls panel and position it above the calibration slide.

Locate the focus position using the blue ND2 filter (around -1000). Test several spots on the slide.

Copy the value from the slide focusing (or the mean if the values differ a bit) into the "Set Slide Z" field and click on the “Set Slide Z*” button to confirm.

Subtract 150 um from the value in the "Set Slide Z" field and set the needle Z position accordingly. Confirm by clicking on “Set Needle Z*”.

Click "Init" on the Imager: CytePicker2 Manual Controls panel to bring the needle close to the slide.

For adjusting the CPZ use the WhiteA+Dark channel

Adjust the CPZ using the arrow buttons below “Adjust Z µm”. Start with 100 or 50 and go down until the image looks like the left picture, then make adjustments in steps of 10, until the CPZ is properly set (right picture)

Type value of Z from Position Panel (should be ~27000) into “Set Standby CPZ” and confirm it by clicking on the “Set Standby CPZ” button.

Adjust the needle circle by clicking on "Fit," then go to "Imager- View - show needle circle" and use mouse right-click to position circle accurately.

Automatic Calibration:

In the CytePicker interface, click on the calibration option with only the channels of interest selected.

!!! If image is blurry do NOT

Load Tissue

Place your slide in the left slide holder position (“Load Slides”).

Move needle to “Rinsing Solution” (from Imager: Point List) and “Flush Needle” 2-3x

Proceed to “Retrieval Solution” and “Fill Needle” with the solution.

Focus using UV (DAPI channel) and locate the spot either manually by clicking on the slide or by choosing a location from Imager: Point List.

“Pick Cell” of interest.

TOP the needle.

6. Put cell in tube

Go to your tube location “Deposit 1/2/3”.

Click on “Deposit Cell”

You can try to image the cells in the tube in a Z= -680 (don’t worry if you don’t see anything)

Repeat: flush, fill, pick, deposit

Remember, do not click right or move the circle when adjusting the cell.

Flush needle after the final picking with MilliQ several times.

Finally, ensure that you drop the needle in the appropriate location as instructed (Needle discard tray) BEFORE TURNING OFF THE INSTRUMENT (THIS IS REALLY REALLY IMPORTANT).

Selecting tissue points from a prescanned slide for picking

Scan the Slide: Begin by scanning the slide using the imaging system.
Use 10x magnification!!!

Locate the Slide in Tissue Results: In Cytehub, navigate to the Tissue results section and find your slide in the list.

Draw and Duplicate Circles: Click on the circle tool to draw an ellipse or any desired shape. Use the "Predefine shape" option to select the shape you want. Click on the image to place the circle. You can duplicate the circle by pressing Ctrl+C and Ctrl+V, allowing you to add multiple circles to the image.

Select and Export ROIs: Go to the right panel and select all the regions of interest (ROIs) that you have created. Click on the small wheel icon to export the selected ROIs to Cytehub.

Import Tissue Annotations: Open the File Explorer within the Imager Main Control interface and choose “Import CyteHub Tissue Annotations”. Navigate to the folder where your scan files are stored. Select the file with the extension ".rcpickmap" and ".metadata" and click "Open" to import the tissue annotations.

Configure Slide Flipping: A pop-up window will appear. Check the checkbox for "Slides are flipped" if applicable to your slide and choose “Picking from Left/Bottom”.

Start Picking Points: Go to the point list, and you can begin picking tissue points based on the annotations. This step involves selecting and marking specific points of interest within the tissue.

General Information Picking

Needle positions

Needle Standby = 150 µm above slide

Needle Init = 700 µm above slide

Needle Touch = needle is lowered slowly from Standby position until it finds tissue on slide

Needle Top = raises needle 
 !!! NEVER mover needle when it is not topped

Procedure to recover ROI when ROIs were deleted

This the “procedure” I have shared with Pablo Siliceo , to help him recovering the ROIS for adding a cycle to a CycIF experiment going with one more staining cycle remaining. He has been successful.

Check the previous scan folder. Each scan should have a .rcjob file that has the scan area and focus points in it. With some effort, it is possible to recreate the scan area from that information. Here are some guidelines.

In Cythub, create a new scan task for the slide ID you want to do another cycle on (like if you are doing this for the first time)

Find the .rcjob file from a previous scan (the one you want to replicate)

Look for the X and Y start positions, the scan width, and the scan height

Copy those into the newly created scan job (linked to the task you created at step 1)

Look also for the focus points – copy those into the newly created scan job

Attempt to scan using Cytefinder ( by using the ID you created at step 1), it should be able to start and scan and create the rcpnl files and the ome.tiff file.

CytePicker II Retrieval and Deposit Parameters

Nominal Z value

Please find here some explanations about the nominal Z value (indicated into your scan protocol / assay).

First, that value cannot change by itself, only if someone edit the protocol.

In a hidden system file, there is a standard Z offset value written, so the system Cythub is using this value to be close to the correct focal point, based on a calibration-slide with a coverslip (tissue at the bottom side, using 20X). These values are currently on your system : left -275 / right -294.

I remember to see values for the left and right slide holders that are indicated on a paper stick on your screen. They may be slightly different because the thickness of my CyteFinder-calibration-slide (that I use for Service) is different than your classic microscope slides.

On top of that, in your assay/scan-protocol in Cytehub, there is the possibility to indicate a “nominal Z value”, which will be added to the value already built into the system file (left -275 / right -294).

· A nominal Z at “zero” means that Cythub is looking for the good Z focal at -275 (on the left side)

· A nominal Z at “-100” means that Cythub is looking for the good Z focal at -375 (on the left side)

· A nominal Z at “+50” means that Cythub is looking for the good Z focal at -225 (on the left side)

Because nobody wants to adjust every time the nominal Z value in the assay, there is an important focus range that will allow the system to find the correct Z value around the indicated Z. A range of 160 means (with a nominal Z at zero) that it will look for the focus -80 and +80 around -275 (on the left side), meaning it will detect the good focal plan if it is somewhere between -195 and -355. If your tissue is not in this range, it will not find good focus points. It might cancel the scan (error) or scan the tissue out of focus.

To make sure your tissue is within this range, you can open Imager and look at the Z value when your tissue is in focus using the 20X.

That means that If in imager you focus on the tissue at -335 (on the left) ideally your nominal Z in your Cytehub assay should be “-60” , the calculation is [-335-(-275)=-60].

This nominal Z value field in your assay allows you to manage your assay for different thickness of the slide+coverslip.

If the nominal Z or the focus range is not correctly setup, Cytehub risks to incorrectly focus on the tissue. If values are “at the limit to be incorrect”, thanks to the focus-range, it might work for a slide , but not for another slide because of a little difference in the thickness.

You shouldn’t need to change the nominal Z for each batch of slides as long as they are the same manufacturer. But if you receive slides from different manufacturers, you may need to adjust your nominal Z.

File management and best practices

Files and Folder Structure

The files related to the assay are located in the "c:\rcpnl tissue" folder.

To access the folder, navigate to the location where the scan is stored.

Look for the folder corresponding to the scan you are working with.

The folder structure may vary, but the file with the ".rcpnl" extension is the raw data file.

Other Relevant Files

"rcjb": This file contains job-related information. If you want to stop the illumination correction/stitching you can create an empty folder and move this in.

"metadata": This file stores metadata, often used before illumination and after stitching.

Additionally, there may be other files or folders depending on the specific experiment or requirements.

Best practices:

Use low exposure times to avoid bleaching (for example 100-200ms)

Keep record of your scans in the dedicated notebook with all details

Backup your files and keep the rcpnl folder clean
